title: Adas polo
subtitle: Persian lentil rice with dates, raisins, and caramelised onions
excerpt: Basmati rice cooked with brown lentils, layered with caramelised onions, plump medjool dates, and raisins warmed in butter with cinnamon and cumin. Sweet, savoury, and aromatic in equal measure, a Persian rice dish that works both as a side with lamb and as a standalone vegetarian centrepiece.
sourceNotes: Adas polo is a traditional Persian rice dish, documented in Margaret Shaida's 'The Legendary Cuisine of Persia' (1992) and Najmieh Batmanglij's 'Food of Life' (1986). It is one of the mixed rice dishes (polo) of Persian cooking, in which the rice is layered with other ingredients before the final steaming stage. The combination of lentils and sweet dried fruit is distinctly Persian.

content.text: Adas polo is built in layers: the parboiled rice and cooked lentils are combined, then layered with a mixture of caramelised onion, butter-warmed dates, and raisins perfumed with cinnamon and cumin. The layering means different bites will have more or less of the sweet fruit element. The lentils should be cooked to just-tender before mixing with the rice. They continue cooking in the steam.

This dish is served alongside lamb or chicken, or eaten on its own. The combination of savoury lentils with sweet dates and raisins is characteristic of Persian cooking, which uses dried fruit and spice in savoury dishes more than any other major culinary tradition. The tahdig (crispy rice base) forms naturally during the final steaming stage.

items.name: Basmati rice
items.prepNote: rinsed and soaked in salted water for 1 hour

content.text: Cook the lentils and parboil the rice

Cook the {{lentils-brown}} in 400 ml of salted water for 20 minutes until just tender but not soft. Drain. Drain the {{basmati-rice}} and parboil in a large pot of boiling salted water for 5 to 6 minutes until just cooked on the outside but firm in the centre. Drain. Mix the lentils gently through the parboiled rice.
Caramelise onions and warm the fruit

Heat the {{extra-virgin-olive-oil}} in a pan and cook the {{onion}} over medium heat for 25 to 30 minutes until deep golden. Remove half. In the same pan, add the {{dates-medjool}}, {{raisins}}, {{cinnamon-ground}}, and {{cumin-ground}} and cook for 3 minutes until the fruit has plumped slightly. Mix with the remaining onion.

In a large non-stick pot, melt the {{unsalted-butter}} over medium-high heat with 2 tablespoons of water. Add half the rice-lentil mixture. Spread the fruit and onion mixture over the top. Add the remaining rice-lentil mixture to cover. Poke holes with a spoon handle. Wrap the lid in a tea towel and cover. Cook on medium-high for 5 minutes then reduce to lowest heat for 30 to 35 minutes. Invert onto a platter or scoop out, arranging the fruit on top.

items.fix: Cook lentils to just-tender. They should have a slight bite. They continue softening in the steam stage
items.symptom: Lentils have turned to mush by the time the rice is done

items.fix: The fruit layer should be sandwiched between two layers of rice, not touching the base. If the base layer is thin, add a pitta round on the bottom first
items.cause: Placed too close to the base of the pot
items.symptom: Dates and raisins burn during steaming

items.fix: The cloth-wrapped lid traps steam. Check the lid is tight and the cloth is not allowing steam to escape around the edges
items.cause: Not enough steam or lid not tight
items.symptom: Rice is dry and separate rather than fluffy

items.fix: Reduce the dates to 5 or 6. A pinch of salt added to the fruit mixture when cooking balances the sweetness
items.cause: Dates very sweet or too many used

content.text: Adas polo belongs to the family of Persian mixed rice dishes. These are the most prized rice dishes in Iranian cooking. The mix of lentils, dried fruit, and warm spice is typical of Persian cuisine. The savoury-sweet pairing goes back through medieval Islamic cooking into ancient Persian tradition. The dish is linked with Yalda night, the winter solstice. Pomegranates and dried fruit feature heavily then. It is also served at funerals. In Persian tradition, funerals feed the community with the most nourishing food on offer.
